title: Dorsal Vagal Shutdown: What It Is and How to Come Back From It
excerpt: Learn what dorsal vagal shutdown is, why your body reacts this way, and effective somatic strategies to regulate and recover your nervous system.
body:
Understanding the Dorsal Vagal Shutdown

When people talk about feeling stuck, numb, or completely shut down in response to overwhelming stress or trauma, they are often describing a state linked to the dorsal vagal shutdown. This physiological response is rooted in the work of Stephen Porges and his groundbreaking Polyvagal Theory, which expands our understanding of the autonomic nervous system beyond the traditional fight, flight, or freeze dichotomy.

The dorsal vagal complex (DVC) is part of the parasympathetic nervous system that can cause immobilization and dissociation—think of the “playing dead” response seen in animals under extreme threat. This shutdown mechanism, while protective in the moment, can become problematic when it persists or is triggered in everyday life, leaving people feeling disconnected from their bodies and emotions.

What Is the Dorsal Vagal Complex?

The vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve, interfacing with the heart, lungs, digestive tract, and more. According to Porges, the vagus has two distinct branches:

  • Ventral vagal complex (VVC): Supports social engagement, calming, and connection.
  • Dorsal vagal complex (DVC): Controls primitive, unmyelinated fibers linked to immobilization and shutdown.

When faced with extreme threat or trauma, and when fight or flight isn’t an option, the dorsal vagal system kicks in to conserve energy by slowing heart rate, reducing metabolic activity, and inducing a freeze or shutdown state (Porges, 2011). This is the body's last-ditch survival strategy.

Signs of Dorsal Vagal Shutdown

  • Feeling numb or disconnected from your body
  • Low energy or exhaustion despite rest
  • Difficulty speaking or engaging socially
  • Sensation of collapse or heavy limbs
  • Digestive issues like shutdown of gut motility
  • Emotional numbing or dissociation

These symptoms reflect a nervous system stuck in a state of immobilization, which can be deeply confusing for people who have tried talk therapy but still feel trapped in their experiences.

Why Does Dorsal Vagal Shutdown Happen?

Our nervous system evolved to protect us from life-threatening situations. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, describes how traumatic events can overwhelm the nervous system, leading to a state where the dorsal vagal system inhibits action to avoid further harm (van der Kolk, 2014).

Trauma, chronic stress, neglect, or overwhelming emotions can all trigger this response. Peter Levine, developer of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body, and the freeze or shutdown state is a physical survival response that needs to be carefully addressed to restore regulation (Levine, 2010).

Gabor Maté highlights how early life adversity sensitizes the nervous system to overreact, making dorsal vagal shutdown more likely even in non-life-threatening situations (Maté, 2018).

How Dorsal Vagal Shutdown Affects Your Body and Mind

When the dorsal vagal system activates, it reduces heart rate and blood pressure, slows breathing, and decreases muscle tone. While this can be lifesaving in the moment, chronic or repeated shutdown can lead to:

  • Emotional numbness: Difficulty accessing feelings or a sense of disconnection from self.
  • Fatigue and low motivation: Persistent exhaustion unrelated to physical exertion.
  • Digestive and immune dysfunction: Because the vagus nerve controls gut motility and immune responses.
  • Cognitive fog and difficulty concentrating: Due to decreased autonomic arousal.

Dan Siegel discusses how nervous system regulation is foundational to mental health, and when regulation is compromised, it affects memory, emotional resilience, and the sense of safety (Siegel, 2012).

Coming Back from Shutdown: Somatic Strategies to Re-regulate

Recovery from dorsal vagal shutdown requires gently re-engaging your nervous system’s regulation mechanisms. This isn’t about forcing yourself to “snap out of it” but about creating safety and connection within your body.

1. Build Safety Through the Ventral Vagal Pathway

Deb Dana, a leading clinician on Polyvagal Theory, stresses the importance of activating the ventral vagal system that supports social engagement and calm states (Dana, 2018). Practices include:

  • Safe social connection: Being with trusted people or supportive therapists.
  • Gentle eye contact and facial expressiveness: Engages the social engagement system.
  • Modulated voice tone: Speaking softly and slowly to yourself or others.

2. Somatic Awareness and Tracking Sensations

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy encourages noticing body sensations without judgment, allowing the nervous system to recalibrate naturally (Ogden, 2015). Start by:

  • Observing physical sensations in a non-threatening way
  • Noticing where tension or numbness shows up
  • Allowing small shifts in sensation without pushing

3. Breathing and Movement Practices

Controlled breathing can stimulate the vagus nerve and shift out of shutdown. Try:

  • Slow diaphragmatic breathing: Inhale for 4, exhale for 6 counts
  • Gentle rocking or swaying: Activates vestibular input to promote regulation
  • Micro-movements: Small, incremental movements to avoid overwhelm

4. Grounding Exercises

Grounding helps bring awareness to the present moment, gently pulling you out of dissociation. Techniques include:

  • Feeling your feet on the floor
  • Noticing textures or temperatures of objects
  • Using your five senses to anchor awareness

5. Trauma-Informed Support

Sometimes professional guidance is essential. Approaches like EMDR (Francine Shapiro, 2017) or Internal Family Systems (Richard Schwartz, 1995) can help process trauma stored in the body and mind, facilitating movement out of shutdown.

“Dorsal vagal shutdown is not a failure of the nervous system but a deeply ingrained survival strategy that requires compassionate, somatic approaches to recover.” – The Oracle Lover

Why Talk Therapy Alone Often Isn’t Enough

Traditional talk therapy engages the cognitive brain but doesn’t always address the body-based imprint of trauma. As Bessel van der Kolk argues, trauma is stored in the body and nervous system, making somatic approaches essential to unlock stuck states like dorsal vagal shutdown (van der Kolk, 2014).

Many who feel stuck despite therapy benefit from integrating body-based work that targets nervous system regulation directly, such as Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed interventions.

Integrating Polyvagal Knowledge Into Daily Life

This knowledge isn’t just for therapy sessions. Stephen Porges encourages applying Polyvagal Theory principles to everyday life by prioritizing safety and connection to promote ventral vagal activation and prevent shutdown (Porges, 2017).

  • Start your day with grounding and breathwork.
  • Schedule regular breaks to check in with your body.
  • Engage in social activities that feel safe and nurturing.
  • Practice self-compassion when shutdown arises instead of judging yourself.

Final Thoughts

Dorsal vagal shutdown is a complex but understandable nervous system response to overwhelming threat or trauma. Recognizing its signs and origins empowers you to engage in somatic strategies that gently support nervous system regulation and healing. The road back from shutdown is not linear, but with patience, somatic awareness, and safe connection, it's possible to reclaim vitality, presence, and a sense of safety in your body and life.

Remember the words of Peter Levine: trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you. Healing requires working with your body’s wisdom to restore balance and resilience (Levine, 2010).

References

  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No. Wiley.
  • Ogden, P. (2015). Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. Norton.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton.
  • Porges, S. (2017). Polyvagal Safety: Attachment, Communication, Self-Regulation. Psychotherapy.
  • Schapiro, F. (2017). EMDR Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. (2012). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.
  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Viking.

faqs.a: Dorsal vagal shutdown is triggered by extreme stress, trauma, or situations where fight or flight responses are unavailable, leading the body to immobilize as a survival mechanism.

faqs.q: How is dorsal vagal shutdown different from freeze or flight responses?
faqs.a: Freeze is an active state of heightened awareness without movement, while dorsal vagal shutdown is a deeper immobilization and dissociation, involving shutdown of bodily functions to conserve energy.

faqs.q: Can I recover from dorsal vagal shutdown on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, through somatic practices like breathwork, grounding, and gentle movement you can support nervous system regulation, but trauma-informed professional support may be necessary for deeper healing.

faqs.q: Why doesn’t talk therapy always help with dorsal vagal shutdown?
faqs.a: Because dorsal vagal shutdown is rooted in the body and nervous system, talk therapy alone often misses the somatic imprint of trauma that needs to be addressed through body-based approaches.
